free_text: Resonance strengths in the N-14(p,g)O-15 and
N-15(p,ag)C-12 reactions.
CIVEN, Venice, Italy
the 3 MV Tandetron accelerator at
Forschungszentrum Dresden-Rossendorf(FZD)
Titanium nitride targets were used.
Four different samples were used, all consisting of a
200 mug/cm**2 thick layer of TiN on a 0.22 mm thick
tantalum backing. The targets were placed tilted by
55 degree with respect to the beam axis and were
directly water cooled.
.The gamma-ray detection system consisted of four
high-purity germanium (HPGe) detectors. Three 100%
(relative efficiency) HPGe detectors with a bismuth
germanate (BGO) escape-suppression shield, surrounded
by a 2 cm thick lead shield and a 10 cm frontal lead
shield with a cone-shaped opening of 3-5 cm diameter
were used-
Two were placed horizontally at 127 degree (left and
right) relative to the beam direction, with front faces
at 32 cm from the target- called Det1 and Det3,
The third was placed at 90 degree directly above the
target, at 28 cm distance-called (Det2).
A fourth, smaller, HPGe detector (Det4, 60% relative
efficiency, no escape suppression, surrounded by a 1cm
thick lead shield) was placed at 4 cm distance from the
target, at downward angle 55 degree
The branching ratios for the decay of the
E(p)=1058 keV resonance in N-14(p,g)O-15 have been
redetermined. The data reported here should facilitate
future studies of off-resonant capture in the
N-14(p,g)O-15 reaction that are needed for an improved
R-matrix extrapolation of the cross section.
S.B

*By authors*.The data points are from the present
experiment, again unfolded by the respective
attenuation coefficients Q2,4 (Table I). The data of
Det1 (-127 degree ) and Det3 (127 degree) are
consistent and have been averaged for this plot
at 127 degree.
.Uncorrected experimental yields ratio
.'MISC' uncertainty.
*By authors*. For calculating the reference yield of
the E(p) =278 keV resonance, the yields of the three
peaks corresponding to the decay of the E-EXC =6792,
6172, and 5182 keV excited states of O-15 and their
precisely known branching ratios were used.
The strength of the E(p) =1058 keV resonance was
obtained based on the yields from the two strongest
transitions, 5241--0 and 8284--0, and the presently
measured branching ratios.
*By authors*.Using these strength ratio(see DATA with
point 3) and the reference strength wg(278)=13.1+-0.6
new absolute resonance strengths have been obtained for
the three resonances under study
